meta.title: Project Memory MCP | AI Project Memory
meta.description: MCP project memory with Supabase persistent context for AI models, OpenCode, Claude Code CLI, Qwen Code, and Codex.
meta.ogDescription: Persistent project memory for AI models and coding agents powered by Supabase.
meta.twitterDescription: AI project memory with Supabase persistent context for OpenCode, Claude Code CLI, Qwen Code, and Codex.

hero.title: Long-term project memory for AI models and coding agents
hero.description: Project Memory MCP stores architecture, decisions, tasks, warnings, preferences, and session state so your AI tools can resume work exactly where they left off.

index.title: Interactive index
index.description: Jump directly to the area you need and keep implementation steps easy to follow.

features.title: Why teams use it
features.intro: Built for teams that want MCP project memory, Supabase persistent context, AI agent memory, and reliable handoff between OpenCode, Claude Code CLI, Qwen Code, Codex, and native sessions.

items.title: Persistent project context
items.text: Store architecture, decisions, tasks, warnings, and preferences in one durable memory layer.

items.title: Cross-client continuity
items.text: Move from OpenCode to Claude Code CLI, Qwen Code, Codex, or native chat without losing where you were.

items.title: Context optimization
items.text: Trim large payloads safely with interface-aware and model-aware token strategies.

items.title: Operational visibility
items.text: Track warnings, sessions, and interface analytics so the next AI step starts with the right memory.

setup.title: Setup in five steps
setup.intro: Minimal setup, keyboard-friendly navigation, and responsive guidance for desktop, tablet, and mobile.
steps: Clone the repository and install Python dependencies.
Create a Supabase project and run the provided schema.sql file.
Copy .env.example to .env and add your public values.
Register the server in mcp.json or your MCP-compatible client settings.
Start the server and verify that project memory resumes correctly across sessions.

usage.title: Use it with natural language
usage.intro: In most MCP-compatible clients, you talk to the model normally and it decides which tool to call when tool use is enabled.
usage.cardOneText: You do not need to manually name a tool for common tasks. Ask for the context, ask what changed, or ask to continue the project.
usage.cardTwoText: If the client exposes Project Memory MCP tools, the model can call `load_unified_context`, `sync_session_state`, or other tools automatically based on your request.
usage.cardThreeTitle: Force a tool only when needed
usage.cardThreeText: Manual tool calls are useful for debugging, integrations, or when you want exact control over inputs and outputs.
usage.promptOne: Resume this project and tell me where we left off.
usage.promptTwo: Load the stored project memory before continuing the refactor.
usage.promptThree: Save this architecture decision and mark the current task as in progress.
usage.note: If a client disables tool use, the model cannot call MCP tools automatically. In that case, enable MCP tools in the client or call the tool explicitly.

clients.title: Configuration
clients.intro: You can keep this repository in the folder you prefer. Most users clone it once, keep a private `.env`, and connect multiple IDEs or AI clients to the same MCP server.
clients.installText: Clone the repository, install dependencies, run `pip install -e .`, create `.env`, run `schema.sql` in Supabase, and keep the folder in a stable location.
clients.envText: The MCP server only needs three values for normal use: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_KEY, and OWNER_ID. DATABASE_URL is optional and only matters if you also want direct Postgres access for admin tasks or SQL tooling.
clients.envSourceText: SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_KEY come from your Supabase project settings. OWNER_ID is not provided by Supabase: you choose a stable identifier such as your GitHub username, company slug, or workspace id and keep using the same value.
clients.platformText: The MCP is not Windows-only. After `pip install -e .`, macOS and Linux expose the same `project-memory-mcp` command, so the same MCP server entry works across platforms.
clients.keepText: Do not copy the server into every project. Keep one stable folder for the MCP server and connect all clients to that same installation.
clients.standardText: After installation, configure clients to launch the same command: project-memory-mcp. This makes the server behave like any other stdio MCP server.
clients.startTitle: 4. Do I need to start it after every reboot?
clients.startText: Usually no. When a client is configured with the `project-memory-mcp` command, it normally starts the server automatically when needed. You only need to run it manually when testing it directly or debugging outside the client.
clients.genericText: If an IDE or agent accepts a standard MCP JSON config with an mcpServers entry, you can usually paste the same server block and only adjust environment values.
clients.opencodeText: Point OpenCode to the shared MCP config or paste the same server block into its settings. PROJECT_MEMORY_INTERFACE is optional if you want to force a client label; most setups can skip it.
clients.codexText: Register the same server block in Codex MCP settings, then launch Codex with that configuration.
clients.claudeDesktopText: Add a local MCP server entry to Claude Desktop using the installed project-memory-mcp command and the same environment variables from your local setup.
clients.claudeCodeText: Run Claude Code with the shared MCP config or the equivalent CLI registration flow.
clients.qwenText: Point Qwen Code to the same MCP config or paste the shared server block into its settings. PROJECT_MEMORY_INTERFACE is optional if you want to force a client label.
clients.antigravityText: Paste the same MCP JSON into Antigravity MCP settings. On Windows, a common config location is shown below.
clients.claudeDesktopPathLinux: Linux: check your local Claude Desktop app data folder

api.title: Core MCP tools
api.intro: The API focuses on loading shared context, saving decisions, syncing session state, and measuring interface usage.

items.name: load_unified_context
items.description: Load durable project memory optimized for the current interface and model.

items.name: save_cross_interface_decision
items.description: Persist a decision so future sessions inherit the same reasoning.

items.name: sync_session_state
items.description: Store the current working state so another AI client can continue the same task.

items.name: get_interface_analytics
items.description: Measure usage patterns across OpenCode, Claude Code CLI, Qwen Code, Codex, and native workflows.

examples.title: Examples by interface
examples.intro: Switch views to compare OpenCode, Claude Code CLI, and Codex workflows using the same project memory backend.

opencode.label: OpenCode
opencode.title: OpenCode workflow
opencode.text: Load project memory before editing so OpenCode inherits architecture, previous decisions, and active warnings.
opencode.commandLabel: Launch OpenCode with the MCP config

claude.label: Claude Code
claude.title: Claude Code CLI workflow
claude.text: Use the same stored memory to resume implementation, debugging, or planning from the last recorded session state.
claude.commandLabel: Launch Claude Code CLI with the MCP config

codex.label: Codex
codex.title: Codex workflow
codex.text: Preserve warnings, tasks, and synced session state when handing off coding work to Codex.
codex.commandLabel: Launch Codex with the MCP config

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.description: This architecture keeps project memory durable while optimization, routing, and interface detection stay inside the MCP server.

seo.title: SEO focus
seo.description: Project Memory MCP, AI project memory, Supabase persistent context, AI agent memory, OpenCode memory, Claude Code CLI memory, Qwen Code memory, and Codex memory are explained in plain language for quick onboarding.

footer.title: Open source memory infrastructure for serious AI workflows
